quiz_questions.question_text: A traditional 401(k) uses _____ contributions.

options.text: After-tax
Pre-tax (reduces taxable income now; taxes are paid upon withdrawal in retirement)
Tax-free at all times
Penalty-free at any age

quiz_questions.explanation: Traditional 401(k) contributions reduce your current taxable income, providing an immediate tax benefit. However, all withdrawals in retirement are taxed as ordinary income.

quiz_questions.question_text: A Roth IRA uses _____ contributions.

options.text: Pre-tax
After-tax (you pay taxes now, but all growth and qualified withdrawals are completely tax-free)
Tax-deductible
Employer-matched only

quiz_questions.explanation: Roth IRA contributions are made with after-tax money, so there is no immediate tax break. The major benefit is that all growth and qualified withdrawals are completely tax-free, making it powerful for young investors.

quiz_questions.question_text: An employer match of 50% up to 6% of salary on a $60,000 salary means the employer contributes:

options.text: $500/year

quiz_questions.explanation: Employee contributes 6% of $60,000 = $3,600. Employer matches 50% of that: $3,600 x 0.50 = $1,800. This is essentially free money — an instant 50% return on the employee's contribution.

quiz_questions.question_text: The 4% rule suggests retirees can annually withdraw _____ from their portfolio.

options.text: 1% of the initial portfolio
4% of the initial portfolio (adjusted for inflation each year) with low risk of running out over 30 years
10% of the portfolio each year
20% of the portfolio each year

quiz_questions.explanation: Based on the Trinity Study, withdrawing 4% of your initial portfolio (adjusting annually for inflation) has historically sustained a balanced portfolio for at least 30 years in roughly 95% of scenarios.

quiz_questions.question_text: $500/month into a 401(k) from age 25 to 65 at 8% grows to approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: Using the FV annuity formula with $500/month at 8%/12 for 480 months gives approximately $1,745,504. Total contributions: $240,000. Compound interest generated: over $1.5 million, or 86% of the final balance.

quiz_questions.question_text: Social Security replaces approximately _____ of pre-retirement income for average earners.

options.text: ~40% (personal savings are critical to fill the gap)

quiz_questions.explanation: Social Security replaces about 40% of pre-retirement income for average earners. Since most retirees need 70-80% of pre-retirement income, personal savings must fill the remaining 30-40% gap.

quiz_questions.question_text: Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs) from traditional retirement accounts start at age:

options.text: 59 and a half
73 (as of the SECURE 2.0 Act)

quiz_questions.explanation: The SECURE 2.0 Act raised the RMD age to 73. Traditional 401(k) and IRA holders must begin withdrawing (and paying taxes on) a minimum amount annually. Roth IRAs have no RMDs during the owner's lifetime.

quiz_questions.question_text: Early withdrawal from a 401(k) before age 59 and a half incurs:

options.text: No penalty
A 10% penalty plus income taxes on the withdrawal
Only income taxes, no penalty
A 5% penalty only

quiz_questions.explanation: Early 401(k) withdrawals face both a 10% early withdrawal penalty AND ordinary income tax. A $10,000 withdrawal for someone in the 22% bracket could cost $3,200 in taxes and penalties, netting only $6,800.

quiz_questions.question_text: Target-date funds automatically:

options.text: Pick individual stocks for maximum return
Shift from aggressive (more stocks) to conservative (more bonds) as the target retirement date approaches
Guarantee a specific retirement income
Eliminate all investment risk

quiz_questions.explanation: A Target Date 2060 fund starts heavily in stocks for growth and gradually shifts to bonds for stability as 2060 approaches. This glide path automates the age-based allocation adjustment most investors should make.

quiz_questions.question_text: The biggest factor in retirement savings is:

options.text: Picking the right investment fund
Starting early — time for compounding is irreplaceable
Timing the market for optimal entry points
Having a high salary

quiz_questions.explanation: Starting at 25 vs. 35 with the same contribution can nearly double the final balance. Time allows compound interest to work, and the earliest contributions have the most time to grow — making each year of delay costly.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'traditional IRA'?

options.text: An employer-sponsored retirement plan
An individual retirement account where contributions may be tax-deductible, but withdrawals in retirement are taxed as ordinary income
A savings account with FDIC insurance
A type of pension plan

quiz_questions.explanation: A traditional IRA allows tax-deductible contributions (subject to income limits), tax-deferred growth, and taxable withdrawals in retirement. It is best for those who expect to be in a lower tax bracket in retirement.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'employer match' mean in a 401(k)?

options.text: The employer deducts money from your paycheck for taxes
Free money from your employer added to your retirement account based on your own contributions
The employer invests in the same stocks as you
The government reimburses your retirement contributions

quiz_questions.explanation: An employer match is additional money contributed to your 401(k) based on your own contributions. A 100% match up to 3% of salary is an instant 100% return on that money — always contribute at least enough to get the full match.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'tax-deferred growth' mean?

options.text: You never pay taxes on investment gains
Investment gains are not taxed annually; taxes are paid only when money is withdrawn, allowing more capital to compound over time
Taxes are paid upfront before any growth occurs
The government defers all taxes for retirees

quiz_questions.explanation: In tax-deferred accounts (401k, traditional IRA), you do not pay taxes on dividends or capital gains each year. This allows the full balance to compound, creating a significant advantage over taxable accounts where annual taxes reduce the growing balance.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'catch-up contribution' mean?

options.text: Contributing to make up for missed employer match payments
Additional contributions above the standard limit allowed for workers age 50 and older ($7,500 extra for 401(k), $1,000 extra for IRA)
Investing more aggressively to compensate for poor returns
Transferring money from a 401(k) to an IRA

quiz_questions.explanation: Catch-up contributions help older workers who may have started saving late. At 50+, you can contribute an extra $7,500 to your 401(k) (total $30,500) and $1,000 extra to your IRA (total $8,000) in 2024.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'Roth conversion'?

options.text: Transferring money from a savings account to a Roth IRA
Moving money from a traditional IRA or 401(k) to a Roth IRA, paying income taxes on the converted amount now for tax-free growth later
Converting stocks to bonds within a retirement account
Closing a Roth IRA and opening a traditional IRA

quiz_questions.explanation: A Roth conversion moves pre-tax retirement funds to a Roth account. You pay income tax on the converted amount now, but all future growth and withdrawals are tax-free. It is most beneficial in low-income years.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'vesting schedule' mean for employer 401(k) matches?

options.text: The investment options available in the plan
The timeline after which employer contributions become fully owned by the employee; leaving before full vesting means forfeiting some employer match
The schedule of when contributions are deducted from paychecks
The enrollment period for the 401(k) plan

quiz_questions.explanation: A vesting schedule determines when employer contributions become yours. A 3-year cliff vest means you own 0% until year 3, then 100%. A graded 6-year vest increases ownership 20% annually from year 2 to year 6.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'Rule of 72' and how does it apply to retirement planning?

options.text: A rule that requires 72% of income to go toward retirement
72 divided by the annual return estimates the years for an investment to double; at 8%, money doubles roughly every 9 years
You need 72 times your monthly expenses saved for retirement
Social Security benefits are calculated at 72% of your highest salary

quiz_questions.explanation: 72 / 8% = 9 years to double. Starting with $50,000 at age 25: doubles to $100K by 34, $200K by 43, $400K by 52, $800K by 61. This simple tool illustrates why starting early and earning reasonable returns creates enormous wealth.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'tax diversification' mean in retirement planning?

options.text: Paying the highest tax rate possible to maximize deductions
Having money in different account types (pre-tax, Roth, taxable) to have flexibility in managing tax liability during retirement
Investing only in tax-exempt municipal bonds
Avoiding all taxable investments

quiz_questions.explanation: Having a mix of pre-tax (401k/traditional IRA), Roth (tax-free), and taxable accounts gives retirees flexibility to control their tax bracket each year by choosing which accounts to withdraw from.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a '401(k) rollover'?

options.text: Automatically increasing your 401(k) contribution each year
Transferring funds from an old employer's 401(k) to a new employer's plan or an IRA without incurring taxes or penalties
Withdrawing 401(k) funds to pay off debt
Converting a 401(k) to a savings account

quiz_questions.explanation: A rollover moves retirement funds from one qualified account to another (401k to IRA, or old 401k to new 401k) without triggering taxes. A direct rollover (trustee-to-trustee) is the safest method.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'sequence of returns risk' mean?

options.text: The risk of choosing investments in the wrong order
The risk that poor market returns in the early years of retirement, combined with withdrawals, permanently deplete the portfolio faster than expected
The order in which you open retirement accounts
The risk of Social Security payments being delayed

quiz_questions.explanation: A major market decline in the first few years of retirement is devastating because withdrawals lock in losses. A $1M portfolio with 4% withdrawal that drops 30% in year 1 becomes $660K, and recovery is extremely difficult.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two employees earn $75,000. Employee A contributes 6% to get the full 50% employer match; Employee B contributes only 3%. Over 30 years at 7%, how much more does Employee A accumulate (including the match)?

options.text: About $50,000 more
About $250,000 more
About $25,000 more
They end up with the same amount

quiz_questions.explanation: A contributes $4,500/yr + $2,250 match = $6,750/yr. B contributes $2,250/yr + $1,125 match = $3,375/yr. At 7% for 30 years: A accumulates ~$680,000 vs. B's ~$340,000. Employee B forfeited ~$340,000 by not capturing the full match.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 25-year-old in the 12% tax bracket contributes $500/month to a Roth IRA at 8% for 40 years. The account grows to ~$1,745,504, all tax-free. If she had used a traditional IRA and withdraws in the 22% bracket, her after-tax amount would be approximately:

options.text: ~$1,745,504 (same as Roth)
~$2,000,000 (traditional is always better)

quiz_questions.explanation: The traditional IRA grows to the same $1,745,504, but withdrawals are taxed at 22%: $1,745,504 x 0.78 = ~$1,361,493 after tax. The Roth saver keeps the full $1,745,504. When your future tax rate is higher, Roth wins decisively.

quiz_questions.question_text: A couple needs $80,000/year in retirement income. Social Security provides $36,000/year combined. Using the 4% rule, they need a retirement portfolio of approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: Income gap: $80,000 - $36,000 = $44,000 needed from savings. Portfolio required: $44,000 / 0.04 = $1,100,000. This calculation shows how Social Security reduces the savings burden but cannot cover the full retirement need.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 50-year-old with $400,000 saved wants to retire at 65 with $1,500,000. Assuming 7% annual returns, approximately how much must she save per month for the next 15 years?

options.text: ~$500/month

quiz_questions.explanation: Existing $400,000 grows to ~$1,103,000 in 15 years at 7%. Remaining gap: $1,500,000 - $1,103,000 = $397,000. Using the FV annuity formula to reach $397,000 in 15 years at 7%: ~$1,250/month. Plus catch-up contributions, total ~$1,950/month.

quiz_questions.question_text: An employee takes a $20,000 early withdrawal from his 401(k) at age 40. He is in the 24% tax bracket. The total cost of the withdrawal (taxes + penalty) is:

options.text: $2,000 (10% penalty only)
$6,800 ($4,800 income tax + $2,000 penalty)
$10,000 (50% total cost)

quiz_questions.explanation: Income tax: $20,000 x 24% = $4,800. Early withdrawal penalty: $20,000 x 10% = $2,000. Total cost: $6,800. He receives only $13,200 of the $20,000. Plus he loses the future growth those funds would have generated.

quiz_questions.question_text: Using the Rule of 72, $100,000 at 8% will double to $200,000 in approximately 9 years. After 36 years (four doublings), the original $100,000 grows to approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: Each doubling multiplies by 2: $100K -> $200K (year 9) -> $400K (year 18) -> $800K (year 27) -> $1.6M (year 36). This exponential growth is why starting early with even modest amounts creates substantial retirement wealth.

quiz_questions.question_text: A retiree has $1,000,000 and withdraws $40,000 in year 1 (4%). The market drops 25% in year 1. The ending balance after the withdrawal and market decline is approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: After withdrawing $40,000: $960,000. After 25% decline: $960,000 x 0.75 = $720,000. This demonstrates sequence of returns risk — the combination of withdrawals and early losses can devastate a retirement portfolio.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two siblings each earn $70,000. Alex contributes 15% to his 401(k) ($10,500/year) starting at age 25. Jordan contributes 15% starting at age 35. At age 65, assuming 8% returns, the difference in their balances is approximately:

options.text: ~$105,000 (just the extra contributions)
They have roughly the same amount

quiz_questions.explanation: Alex contributes for 40 years ($420,000 total) and accumulates ~$2,750,000. Jordan contributes for 30 years ($315,000 total). The 10-year head start means Alex has ~$1,600,000 more, despite contributing only $105,000 more.

quiz_questions.question_text: A worker earning $55,000 has the following retirement savings options: 401(k) with 100% match up to 3%, Roth IRA (max $7,000), additional 401(k) beyond the match. The optimal contribution order is:

options.text: Max out the Roth IRA first, then contribute to 401(k)
Contribute 3% to 401(k) (capture full match), then max Roth IRA ($7,000), then increase 401(k) beyond 3%
Put everything in a taxable brokerage account
Only contribute to the 401(k), skip the Roth IRA entirely

quiz_questions.explanation: Step 1: Get the free money (100% match on 3% = instant 100% return). Step 2: Roth IRA for tax-free growth. Step 3: Additional 401(k) contributions for tax-deferred growth. This order maximizes both immediate and long-term tax benefits.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 30-year-old earning $60,000 saves 10% of salary ($6,000/year) with a 50% employer match on the first 6% ($1,800 match). Total annual savings: $7,800. At 7% for 35 years, the projected retirement balance is approximately:

options.text: ~$273,000 (just the contributions)

quiz_questions.explanation: $7,800/year (or $650/month) at 7% for 35 years grows to approximately $1,070,000. Total contributions: $273,000. The employer match contributed $63,000 of that, which grew to roughly $135,000 — free money that compounded significantly.
